ributed networks include attributed graphs, attributed hyper-
raphs, and attributed multiplex graphs. Given an attributed
etwork N, node clustering is an important task in graph
ining, which aims to divide the n nodes of N into k disjoint

nds important applications in biological analysis [1], online

In this work, we present ANCKA, an effective and efficient
ttributed network clustering method that is versatile to sup-
ort attributed hypergraph clustering (AHC), attributed graph
lustering (AGC), and attributed multiplex graph clustering
AMGC). ANCKA subsumes our previous work AHCKA [6] that
s dedicated to AHC. In what follows, we first elaborate on
HC and then generalize to AGC and AMGC.
In a hypergraph, each edge can join an arbitrary number
f nodes, referred to as a hyperedge. The hyperedge allows
precise description of multilateral relationships between

2
nodes, such as collaboration relationships of multiple au-  t
thors of a paper, interactions among proteins [7], products  w
purchased together in one shopping cart, transactions involv-
ing multiple accounts [8]. In practice, nodes in hypergraphs  p
are often associated with many attributes, e.g., the academic  w
profile of authors and the descriptive data of products. The  s
AHC problem is to divide the n nodes in such an attributed
i
hypergraph into k disjoint clusters such that nodes within
C
the same cluster are close to each other with high connected-  A
ness and homogeneous attribute characteristics. AHC finds  a
numerous real-life applications in community discovery [9],  t
organization structure detection [1], Web query analysis [5],  n
biological analysis [10], etc. As another example, AHC can  o
cluster together academic publications with high relevance by  a
considering co-authorship hyperedges and keyword attributes  h
in academic hypergraphs [11].  o
Effective AHC computation is a highly challenging task,  n
especially for large attributed hypergraphs with millions of  t
nodes. First, nodes, hyperedge connections, and attributes  t
are heterogeneous objects with inherently different traits,  j
whose information cannot be seamlessly integrated in a sim-  n
ple and straightforward way. Second, as observed in previous  a
works on simple graphs [12, 13], higher-order relationships  h
between nodes and node-attribute associations are crucial for  a
clustering. However, computing such multi-hop relationships  g
and associations via hyperedges usually with more than two  c
nodes in attributed hypergraphs is rather difficult due to the  f
complex hypergraph structures and prohibitive computational  m
2
overheads (up to O(n ) in the worst case).  i
In the literature, a plethora of clustering solutions [14–16]  C
are developed for plain hypergraphs. These methods overlook  m
attribute information, leading to severely compromised AHC  o
result quality. Besides, a large body of research on attributed  o
graph clustering is conducted, resulting in a cornucopia of  i
efficacious techniques [2, 13]. However, most of these works  A
cannot be directly applied to handle large attributed hyper-
graphs with more complex and unique structures. Inspired by  a
the technical advances in the above fields, a number of efforts  s
have been made towards AHC computation in the past years.  [
The majority of AHC methods rely on non-negative matrix  n
factorization [1, 5], which requires numerous iterations of  t
expensive matrix operations and even colossal space costs  g
of materializing n×n dense matrices. Particularly, none of  r
them take into account the higher-order relationships between  d
nodes, thereby limiting their result utility. The state-of-the-art  f
approach GRAC [11] extends graph convolution [17] to hyper-  g
graphs, indirectly incorporating higher-order relationships  m
of nodes and attributes for clustering. Notwithstanding its  1
2
enhanced clustering quality, GRAC runs in O(n ) time as an  s
aftermath from costly graph convolution and SVD operations,  n
which is prohibitive for large hypergraphs.

To recapitulate,  a
existing AHC approaches either yield sub-optimal clustering  [
results or incur tremendous computational costs, rendering  a

hem impractical to cope with large attributed hypergraphs
ith millions of nodes.
Given the above, can we combine and orchestrate hy-
ergraph topology and attribute information in an optimized
ay for improved clustering quality while achieving high
calability over large attributed hypergraphs? We offer a pos-
tive answer by presenting AHCKA (Attributed Hypergraph
lustering via K-nearest neighbor Augmentation), a novel
HC approach that significantly advances the state of the
rt in AHC computation. AHCKA surpasses existing solutions
hrough several key techniques. The first one is a K-nearest
eighbor (KNN) augmentation scheme, which augments the
riginal hypergraph structure with a KNN graph containing
dditional connections constructed by adjacent nodes with K
ighest attribute similarities. This is inspired by a case study
n a real dataset manifesting that incorporating all-pairwise
ode connections via attributes or none of them jeopardizes
he empirical clustering quality. Second, AHCKA formulates
he AHC task as a novel optimization problem based on a
oint random walk model that allows for the seamless combi-
ation of high-order relationships from both the hypergraph
nd KNN graph. Further, AHCKA converts the original NP-
ard problem into an approximate matrix trace optimization
nd harnesses efficient matrix operations to iteratively and
reedily search for high-quality solutions. Lastly, AHCKA in-
ludes an effective initialization method that considerably
acilitates the convergence of the optimization process using
erely a handful of iterations. We conduct extensive exper-
ments on attributed hypergraph data in different domains.
ompared with baselines, AHCKA exhibits superior perfor-
ance in both clustering quality and efficiency. For instance,
n the Amazon dataset with 2.27 million nodes, AHCKA gains
ver 10-fold speedup and a significant improvement of 4.8%
n clustering accuracy compared to state-of-the-art. Our work
HCKA has been published in [6].
In addition to attributed hypergraphs, attributed graphs
nd attributed multiplex graphs are prevalent in real-world
cenarios, such as social networks [18] and citation networks
19]. Different from hypergraphs that allow more than two
odes to form an edge, in a graph, an edge connects exactly
wo nodes. A multiplex graph consists of multiple layers of
raphs with a shared set of nodes, and different graph layers
epresent node connections from different perspectives or
omains, e.g., different types of relationships or relations
ormed in different time frames or spaces [18, 19]. Attributed
raph clustering (AGC) is one of the most significant graph
ining problems, extensively studied in the literature [2,
3], with many applications, e.g., community detection in
ocial networks [20] and functional cartography of metabolic
etworks [21]. Furthermore, a rich collection of studies on
ttributed multiplex graph clustering (AMGC) also exists in
22–25], to support important applications, e.g., biological
nalysis [19], community detection [18] and social analysis.

.2 (α,β, γ)-RandomWalk
andom walk with restart [31] (RWR) is one of the most
ommon and effective models for capturing the multi-hop
elationships between nodes in a graph [32], and is widely
sed in many tasks such as ranking [31, 33], recommendation
34], and clustering [35]. Given a graph G, a source node u
nd a stopping probability α (typically α = 0.2), at each step,
n RWR originating from u either stops at the current node
ith probability α, or randomly picks an out-neighbor v of
he current node according to the weight of edge (u, v) and
avigates to v with the remaining 1−α probability. It follows
hat RWR score (a.k.a. personalized PageRank [36]) of any
ode pair (u, v) represents the probability that an RWR from
ends at node v. Intuitively, two nodes with dense (one-hop
r multi-hop) connections should have a high RWR score.
Nevertheless, RWR is designed for general graphs, and
hus cannot be directly applied to our augmented hypergraph
A as it consists of a hypergraph HO and a general graph
K. We devise a joint random walk scheme, named (α,β, γ)-
andom walk, which conducts the RWR process over HO and
K jointly to seamlessly integrate topological proximity over
oth networks.

On large attributed networks, e.g., Amazon and MAG-PM hy-
pergraphs, each with more than 2 million nodes, as reported
in Table 7, AHCKA with 16 CPU threads still needs 1286s
and 1372s respectively for clustering, despite its superior ef-
ficiency compared with baselines. Moreover, AHCKA does not
exhibit acceleration proportional to increased CPU threads.
As shown in Figure 6, when the number of CPU threads is
raised from 1 to 32, the time drops from around 3000s to
1200s, with a speedup of merely 2.5 (Amazon) or 2.7 (MAG-
PM). In particular, increasing the number of threads from 16
to 32 provides rather limited acceleration (less than 10%).
To overcome the limitation of CPU parallelization, we
resort to the massive parallel processing power of GPUs
(graphical processing unit) and develop ANCKA-GPU to boost
efficiency, with about one order of magnitude speedup on
large networks with millions of nodes in experiments. For ex-
ample, ANCKA-GPU only needs 120s on an MAG-PM dataset,
over 10 times faster than the 1372s of ANCKA. Compared to
CPUs, the design of GPUs enables them to leverage numer-
ous threads to handle data processing simultaneously, which
is beneficial for vector and matrix operations at scale. Please
see [45] for details on GPU computing.

graph layers: co-author, co-conference (publishing at the
same conference), and co-term (sharing common key terms).
ACM and DBLP-MG have research areas labeled as ground
truth clusters, while IMDB is labeled by movie genres.

Hypergraph Clustering. Motivated by the applications in  s
circuit manufacturing, partitioning algorithms have been de-  a
veloped to divide hypergraphs into partitions/clusters, such  t
as hMetis [65] and KaHyPar [14]. These methods typically  i
adopt a three-stage framework consisting of coarsening, ini-  s
tial clustering, and refinement stages. These algorithms di-  l
rectly perform clustering on a coarsened hypergraph with  e
a relatively small size. In addition, they run a portfolio of  p
clustering algorithms and select the best outcome. These al-  t
gorithms rely on a set of clustering heuristics and lack the  e
extensibility for exploiting node attribute information. Hy-  i
pergraph Normalized Cut (HNCut) [52] is a conductance  c
measure for hypergraph clusters from which the normal-  w
ized hypergraph Laplacian ∆ = I−Θ is derived for spectral  o

clustering, where Θ =DV  H DE HDV  . Alternatively,  b
hGraclus [5] optimizes the HNCut objective using a multi-  u
level kernel K-means algorithm. Non-negative matrix factor-  a
ization has also been applied to hypergraph clustering [15].  a
Despite the theoretical soundness, these algorithms are less  a
efficient than the aforementioned partitioning algorithms and  A
they do not utilize node attributes either. For the problem of  t
hypergraph local clustering, which is to find a high-quality  n
cluster containing a specified node, a sweep cut method is  t
proposed [66] to find the cluster based on hypergraph Per-  d
sonalized PageRank (PPR) values. In this paper, we focus on  s
global clustering, a different problem from local clustering.  g
Attributed Hypergraph Clustering. There exist studies  f
designing dedicated clustering algorithms on attributed hy-  F
pergraphs. JNMF [1] is an AHC algorithm based on non-  t
negative matrix factorization (NMF). With normalized hy-  a
pergraph Laplacian [52] matrix ∆ = I−Θ and attribute ma-  A
trix X, JNMF optimizes the following joint objective that  l
includes a basic NMF part as well as a symmetric NMF part:  e

lgorithm is originally proposed for high dimensional data
lustering, while the authors of [11] extend its objective with
he hypergraph normalized Laplacian [52] so that it spawns
aseline methods for AHC. Although NMF-based algorithms
ometimes produce clusters with good quality, their scala-
ility is underwhelming as shown in our experiments. As
he state-of-the-art algorithm for attributed hypergraph clus-
ering, GRAC [11] performs hypergraph convolution [46] on
ode attributes, which resembles the hypergraph diffusion
rocess with mediators [67]. Then clusters are predicted from
he propagated features via a spectral algorithm.
ttributed Graph Clustering. There exists a collection of
tudies on attributed graph clustering. Some studies perform
ttributed graph clustering by adopting probabilistic models
o combine graph structure with attributes, including discrim-
native models such as PCL-DC [68] and generative models
uch as BAGC [2]. Nevertheless, these methods are typically
imited to handling categorical attributes. Moreover, infer-
n
nce over the probability distribution of O(2 ) hyperedges
oses a significant challenge against their generalization
o hypergraph. GNMF [60] is an NMF-based algorithm that
nhances performance by modifying the Laplacian regular-
zer used in traditional NMF to utilize the Laplacian matrix
onstructed from the graph structure. Within the random
alk framework, SA-Cluster [12] algorithm augments the
riginal graph with virtual nodes representing each possi-
le attribute-value pair and performs k-Medroids clustering
sing a random walk distance measure. ACMin [13] defines
ttributed random walk by adding virtual attribute nodes
s bridges and combines it with graph random walk into
joint transition matrix. In a fashion similar to GCN [17],
GCGCN [50] performs graph convolution on node attributes
o produce smooth feature representations that incorporate
etwork structure information and subsequently applies spec-
ral clustering. For their spectral algorithm, the authors also
esign heuristics to prevent propagated features from over-
moothing that undermines cluster quality.

GRACE [27] adopts
raph convolution on node attributes to fuse all available in-
ormation and perform a spectral algorithm based on GRAC [11].
GC [62] exploits both node features and structure informa-
ion via graph convolution and applies spectral clustering on
fine-grained graph that encodes higher-order relations.
ttributed Multiplex Graph Clustering. Via unsupervised
earning on attributed multiplex graphs, neural network mod-
ls can learn node embeddings for clustering, e.g., O2MAC
25] and HDMI [24]. GRACE [27] constructs a multiplex graph
aplacian and uses this matrix for graph convolution. Other
ethods find a single graph that encodes the node proximity
elations in all graph layers and attributes. MCGC [22] per-
orms graph filtering on attributes and learns a consensus
raph leveraging contrastive regularization, while MAGC [23]
xploits higher-order proximity to learn consensus graphs
ithout deep neural networks..

This paper presents ANCKA, a versatile, effective, and effi-
cient attributed network clustering method for AHC, AGC,  1
and AMGC computation. The improvements of ANCKA over
existing solutions in terms of efficiency and effectiveness
is attributed to: (i) an effective KNN augmentation strategy
to exploit useful attribute information, (ii) a novel problem  1
formulation based on a random walk model, and (iii) an
efficient iterative optimization framework with speedup tech-
niques. To further boost the efficiency, we leverage GPUs and  1
develop ANCKA-GPU that is faster than its CPU-parallel coun-
terpart ANCKA on large datasets, while retaining high cluster
quality. We conduct extensive experiments over real-world  1
data to validate the outstanding performance of our methods.
In the future, we plan to extend ANCKA to cope with evolving
attributed networks and enhance its scalability via distributed  1
KNN construction and matrix computation.
